Topic: To what extent should a Central Bank prioritize reaching a 2% inflation target if the high-interest rates required to get there risk triggering a significant recession?
Keynes — Judgement:
FRAMING
The orthodox fixation on a precise 2% target mistakes the map for the territory — it sacrifices the living reality of employment and output on the altar of a statistical abstraction.

REFRAME
When I surveyed the wreckage of the 1920s, I learned that rigid monetary policy pursuing arbitrary price-level targets courts catastrophe. The question is not whether inflation sits at 2% or 3%, but whether we have full employment and productive investment. High interest rates that strangle enterprise and throw millions out of work inflict far graver social damage than modestly elevated prices. The real scandal is not inflation at 3% — it is deliberate unemployment manufactured by central bankers treating economics like geometry. In the long run we are all dead; what matters is whether people have work today, whether businesses can invest, whether animal spirits are encouraged or crushed.

VERDICT
A central bank should abandon the 2% fetish entirely when achieving it requires mass unemployment.

*Better a living economy at 3% inflation than a graveyard of idle men at 2%.*
Hayek — Judgement:
The obsession with a precise 2% target reveals the fatal conceit I warned against—the belief that monetary authorities possess the knowledge to fine-tune such complex phenomena. This number is arbitrary theatre, not economic law.

What matters is not hitting an artificial target but maintaining the predictability of the monetary framework itself. When central banks manufacture recession to reach their proclaimed number, they demonstrate exactly the kind of constructive rationalism that ignores spontaneous market adjustments. The economy is not a machine with dials to calibrate. Real harm—unemployment, business failures, destroyed capital—is not justified by statistical aesthetics. If achieving 2% rather than 3% inflation requires deliberately impoverishing millions, the target itself has become destructive dogma. The central bank's proper role is providing a stable monetary framework, not pursuing numerical precision that pretends to knowledge it cannot possess.

*The pretense of knowledge that justifies widespread suffering in service of an arbitrary number is precisely the hubris that destroys free societies.*
